extra.reasoning:
The property is globally(opted_out implies not sent). A violation requires a reachable state with opted_out=true AND sent=true.

Two decisive structural facts:
1. opted_out is monotonic. The only transition touching it, [t_optout], sets opted_out:=true. No transition ever sets opted_out:=false. So once opted_out becomes true it stays true forever.
2. sent:=true occurs in exactly ONE transition: [t_send_allowed] on send_attempt, guarded by 'if not opted_out'. Every other transition either sets sent:=false or leaves sent unchanged.

Consequently, sent can only be set true while opted_out is false. The act of opting out ([t_optout]) simultaneously sets sent:=false. After opt-out, opted_out can never revert to false, so the guard 'not opted_out' on [t_send_allowed] is permanently false — a send_attempt while opted out instead matches [t_send_blocked], which sets sent:=false. There is no path that sets sent:=true while opted_out is true.

The stage/combination-lock machinery (key_0/key_1/key_2 advancing or resetting stage in 0->1->2) is irrelevant to the property: none of the adv_/rst_ transitions ever set sent:=true; they only set sent:=false (or change stage). They cannot create or preserve a sent=true value across an opt-out.

Therefore every reachable state satisfies opted_out implies not sent. The property holds; no counterexample exists within (or beyond) the 5-event bound.
